real_meaning: This pattern expresses that you have had a certain experience at least once in your life up to now — it's about the existence of a past experience in your personal history, not about something you regularly do. The feeling is like saying 'that experience exists in my life story.' It often carries a mild sense of 'believe it or not' or sharing something noteworthy about yourself.

formation.rule: Attach ことがある to the plain past (た-form) of a verb. Works with all verb types: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べた＋ことがある→<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べたことがある (ru-verb), <ruby>行<rt>い</rt></ruby>った＋ことがある→<ruby>行<rt>い</rt></ruby>ったことがある (u-verb), した＋ことがある (する-verb), きたことがある (くる-verb).
formation.common_mistake: Using the dictionary form instead of the た-form: ✗ <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べることがある (this means 'there are times when I eat' — a completely different grammar point!). ✓ <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べたことがある.

sentences.jp: <ruby>富士山<rt>ふじさん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>登<rt>のぼ</rt></ruby>ったことあるよ。めっちゃきつかった。
sentences.translation: I've climbed Mt. Fuji before, you know. It was super tough.
sentences.notes: In casual speech, the が is often dropped, giving ことある instead of ことがある — this is totally natural and common among friends.

sentences.jp: <ruby>子<rt>こ</rt></ruby>どものころ、<ruby>外国<rt>がいこく</rt></ruby>に<ruby>住<rt>す</rt></ruby>んだことがあります。
sentences.translation: When I was a child, I lived abroad for a time.
sentences.notes: The phrase <ruby>子<rt>こ</rt></ruby>どものころ sets the life-experience frame naturally — たことがある is often paired with a time reference to anchor the experience in your personal history.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>技術<rt>ぎじゅつ</rt></ruby>は、<ruby>以前<rt>いぜん</rt></ruby>にも<ruby>検討<rt>けんとう</rt></ruby>されたことがあります。
sentences.translation: This technology has been considered before as well.
sentences.notes: たことがある can attach to passive forms too — <ruby>検討<rt>けんとう</rt></ruby>されたことがある — making it useful in formal or written contexts when discussing precedents.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>のことを<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きだったことがある。
sentences.translation: There was a time when I liked him.
sentences.notes: Surprises learners because it attaches to the past-tense form of a na-adjective predicate (だった), not a verb — it works to describe a past state that existed in your history, not just a past action.

confused_with.contrast: These two patterns look almost identical but mean completely different things. ～たことがある is about a past experience that exists in your life history (at least once, ever), while ～ることがある means 'there are times when ~' — describing something that happens occasionally on a recurring basis. Minimal pair: <ruby>遅刻<rt>ちこく</rt></ruby>したことがある means 'I have been late before (at some point in my life),' while <ruby>遅刻<rt>ちこく</rt></ruby>することがある means 'I am sometimes late (it happens occasionally).' Choose ～たことがある when you're vouching for the existence of a past experience; choose ～ることがある when you're describing a habitual or occasional pattern.

bunpro_tip: Lock in the mnemonic: た (past) + こと (thing/fact) + がある (exists) = 'the fact of having done it EXISTS in my past.' If you see ことがある without た before it, it's a different grammar point entirely — the た is the whole game here.
